content.full_text:
Frrn Pollrtcrl PnacrtcBs ColtnntssloN
P.O. Box 807 . l2ft J Street 'Stcramcnto. C.\ 95ttll-01t07
(916) -122-5660 ' Ftx (916) -122-0886
Jvne27,2000
Janice Rutherford
Post Office Box2552
Fontana, California 9233 5
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-00-137
Dear Ms. Rutherford:
This letter is in response to your request for advice about the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").1
QUESTION
As a planning commissioner and a candidate for the city council, would a disclaimer in
your written solicitations for campaign contributions prevent you from inadvertently soliciting a
participant in a proceeding before your agency in violation of Section 84308?
CONCLUSION
For purposes of Section 84308, you will not be "soliciting" a contribution from a
participant, unless you know or have reason to know that the person from whom you are
requesting a contribution is a participant. Once you know or have reason to know that someone
is a participant in a proceeding pending before your agencY, You may not solicit that person for a
contribution while the proceeding is pending and for three months thereafter. A disclaimer in a
written solicitation does not negate the solicitation. However, the disclaimer may help you avoid
disqualifi cation in future proceedings.
I Government Code sections 81000 - 91015. Commission regulations appear attitle2, sections 18109 -
18996, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-00-137
Page2
FACTS
You are a member of the Fontana Planning Commission. You are also a candidate for the
Fontana City Council. As a plaruring commissioner, you receive your agenda packets for
meetings two to three days before each meeting. City staff does not inform you of what items
are pending in the application process, or what matters will be on the agenda in the future. Nor
does city staff tell you who has attempted to influence pending matters. Furthermore, you would
have no knowledge of a person who may be influencing other members of the planning
commission outside of the public meetings.
Given the number of people who may be "participants" in a decision before the planning
commission, you intend to include a disclaimer on your written solicitations for contributions:
"Please disregard this request and do not consider it a solicitation
for a contribution if you have any proceedings pending before the
Fontana Planning Commission. California Government Code
Section 84308 prohibits a planning commissioner from soliciting
or receiving a campaign conkibution from any parry or participant
in any issue pending before the commission while that matter is
pending and for three months thereafter."
AI\ALYSN
A. Applicable Law
Section 84308 prohibits elected and appointed members of boards and commissions from
soliciting or receiving a contribution of more than $250 from aparty or participant in specified
proceedings that are pending before the agency, and for three months thereafter. (Section
8430S(b).) In addition, it requires board members and commissioners to recuse themselves from
participating in these proceedings if they have received a contribution of more than $250 from a
party or participant within 12 months before the decision. (Section 8a308(c).) Finally, it
requires board members and commissioners to disclose such contributions on the record of the
proceeding. (Ibid.)
For purposes of Section 84308, an offrcial "solicits" a contribution only when the official:
(1) knows or has reason to know that the person being solicited is a parfy or a participant, or an
agent of a party or a participant, and (2) personally solicits the contribution orally or in writing,
or an agent of the official solicits the contribution with the official's knowledge. (Regulation
1 8438.6(c), copy enclosed.)


File No. A-00-137
Page 3
An official knows or has reason to know about a proceeding pending before his or her
agency if the official: (1) has received an agenda of the proceeding; or (2) has actual knowledge
of the proceeding. (Regulation 18438.7O).) In addition, an official "knows or has reason to
know" that a person is a participant in a proceeding if that person reveals facts in his or her
written or oral support or opposition before the agency that make the person's financial interest
apparent. (Regulation 1 8a38.7(a)(2).)
Accordingly, if you send a written request for a campaign contribution to a participant,
you will not be "soliciting" that participant for purposes of Section 84308 unless: (1) you have
received the agenda for the proceeding, or otherwise have actual knowledge of the proceeding;
and (2) you have received a participant's written or oral comments, or otherwise have actual
knowledge that a person is a participant in a proceeding.
In addition, a solicitation does not include a mass mailing sent to members of the public.
(Regulation 18438.6(d).) A "mass mailing" means more than 200 substantially similar pieces of
mail. (Section 82041.5.) A mass mailing is considered to be "sent to members of the public"
only if it is not addressed to any specific person. For example, a mass mailing addressed to
"Resident" would not be considered a solicitation even if one of the persons receiving the
mailing is a participant.
B. Solicitation Letter with Disclaimer
Under Section 84308, if you know or have reason to know that a person is a participant in
a proceeding pending before your agency, /ou may not solicit campaign contributions from that
person. Once you have requested a contribution from someone you know or have reason to
know is a participant, you have solicited the contribution in violation of Section 84308. A caveat
after the solicitation has already occurred does not negate the solicitation. Thus, the disclaimer
in your written solicitation does not undo the solicitation. Therefore, once you know or have
reason to know that someone is a participant in a proceeding pending before your agency, you
must delete the name of that person from your mailing list while the proceeding is pending, and
for three months thereafter.
Although the disclaimer does not undo the solicitation, you may still find it useful to
include in your fundraising letter. With the disclaimer, you can avoid disqualification in future
proceedings on the basis that you received a campaign contribution from a participant within 12
months before the proceeding. If, however, you do receive a contribution from a participant, you
can still participate in the proceeding if you return the contribution within 30 days from the time
you knew or should have known that the contributor was a participant in the proceeding.
(Section 8a308(c).)


File No. A-00-137
Page 4
For further guidance, I have enclosed a fact sheet on Section 84308. If you have any
other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel
OP
By:
Julia Bilaver
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
LM:JB:tls
Enclosures
A:\rutherford.wpd

participant in a proceeding before your agency in violation of Section 84308?

disqualifi cation in future proceedings.

QUESTION: As a planning commissioner and a candidate for the city council, would a disclaimer in
your written solicitations for campaign contributions prevent you from inadvertently soliciting a
participant in a proceeding before your agency in violation of Section 84308?

CONCLUSION: For purposes of Section 84308, you will not be "soliciting" a contribution from a
participant, unless you know or have reason to know that the person from whom you are
requesting a contribution is a participant. Once you know or have reason to know that someone
is a participant in a proceeding pending before your agencY, You may not solicit that person for a
contribution while the proceeding is pending and for three months thereafter. A disclaimer in a
written solicitation does not negate the solicitation. However, the disclaimer may help you avoid
